name: fine wine
shortDescription: Fine wine with a clear color, patient aroma, and the expensive calm of a bottle that has been stored properly. It belongs at treaty tables, winter feasts, and any dinner where the host wants generosity to look educated.
flavorText: “Fine wine is grape, time, and the courage to wait.”

name: rough moonshine
shortDescription: Rough moonshine is clear, fierce, and usually served with a warning disguised as a grin. It warms the chest, burns the throat, cleans questionable tools, and makes poor judgment feel briefly well funded.
flavorText: “Moonshine is what happens when patience loses to a barrel and a dare.”

name: rough ale
shortDescription: Rough ale is cloudy, hearty, and honest about its lack of refinement. Farmhands, caravan guards, and tired dock crews drink it because it is cheap, filling, and unlikely to ask whether the day deserved better.
flavorText: “Rough ale does not impress the tongue; it reports for work.”

name: rough mead
shortDescription: Rough mead is honey drink without courtly manners, sweet at first and raw around the edges. It is made for harvest nights, loud songs, and cups lifted by people who prefer warmth over polish.
flavorText: “Even poor honey can remember flowers after enough fermenting.”

name: rough wine
shortDescription: Rough wine tastes of bruised fruit, hurried barrels, and a maker who sold before wisdom arrived. It is harsh, useful, and popular wherever thirst and coin both run low before sunset.
flavorText: “Rough wine is less a vintage than an agreement not to complain.”

name: fabled special brew
shortDescription: A fabled special brew with a color that refuses to settle and a scent that suggests several laws were ignored during fermentation. Its recipe is argued over by tavern masters, alchemists, and survivors who remember the next morning differently.
flavorText: “Nobody knows what makes it special. Everyone remembers when it was.”

name: flag of the exiled order
shortDescription: The flag of the exiled order is worn at the edges but still severe in its symbols, a banner for people who lost land before they lost purpose. It sells poorly in loyal towns and very well in rooms where names are spoken carefully.
flavorText: “Exile does not erase a banner; it only teaches it to travel folded.”

name: flag of Goldreef
shortDescription: The flag of Goldreef is bright with coastal pride, trade wealth, and the confidence of a harbor that counts ships before stars. Merchants hang it over cargo to suggest safe dealing, or at least profitable dealing.
flavorText: “Goldreef learned long ago that a flag can be an invitation and a warning.”

name: flag of the island republic
shortDescription: The flag of the island republic carries the clean geometry of councils, sailors, and arguments won by vote instead of crown. It is popular among captains who enjoy declaring independence just before discussing docking fees.
flavorText: “An island flag flies best when surrounded by water and opinions.”

name: flag of the Crownfall dynasty
shortDescription: The flag of the Crownfall dynasty bears the old confidence of inherited rule and polished ceremony. Even folded, it seems to expect trumpets, witnesses, and someone nearby to remember the proper order of titles.
flavorText: “Dynasties fade slowly, but their banners keep practicing.”

name: scarce flag of the old Crownfall empire
shortDescription: A scarce flag of the old Crownfall empire, faded by age but still heavy with conquest, tax roads, and vanished borders. Collectors prize it, rebels hate it, and historians touch the cloth as if it might still issue commands.
flavorText: “Old empires die in maps first, then in songs, and last in fabric.”

name: flag of the Redvale empire
shortDescription: The flag of the Redvale empire is bold, martial, and difficult to display casually. Its colors suit parade lines and fortress walls, though merchants know a banner that inspires fear can also complicate border tolls.
flavorText: “Redvale banners do not flutter; they occupy the wind.”

name: flag of the Border March
shortDescription: The flag of the Border March is practical, weathered, and meant for watchtowers more than palaces. It speaks of toll gates, hard roads, muddy patrols, and the stubborn pride of people who live where maps start arguing.
flavorText: “On the Border March, a flag is less decoration than proof someone is still holding.”

name: flag of the western nations
shortDescription: The flag of the western nations combines distant colors into a single diplomatic cloth, neat enough to hide centuries of disagreement. Envoys use it when cooperation must look easier than it has ever been.
flavorText: “A shared flag is often the prettiest way to postpone an argument.”

name: sign of resistance
shortDescription: A sign of resistance marked for quick display, secret meetings, and walls where fear has stayed too long. It may be crude, but crude things carry well when hands are shaking and time is short.
flavorText: “Resistance begins as a whisper, then learns to be painted.”

name: loaf
shortDescription: A plain loaf of bread with a browned crust and soft interior, the dependable heart of roadside meals. It is not fancy, but few goods move faster when a market smells of rain and hungry travelers.
flavorText: “A loaf is civilization that can be sliced.”

name: baguette
shortDescription: A long crusty baguette with a golden shell and soft, fragrant middle, best sold early while the bread still remembers the oven. Simple food, but powerful on a cold road; few travelers bargain well on an empty stomach.
flavorText: “Bread is never just bread when the road has been long enough.”

name: bun
shortDescription: A bun is small, round, and eager to become part of a meal larger than itself. Bakers sell them by the basket because one feels like a snack, two like prudence, and six like someone planning properly.
flavorText: “Buns are humble until butter arrives.”

name: croissant
shortDescription: A croissant is folded butter and flour made into a crescent of delicate arrogance. It flakes at the first bite, leaves evidence everywhere, and somehow convinces the buyer this mess is sophistication.
flavorText: “The croissant proves that breakfast can be both fragile and smug.”

name: roll
shortDescription: A simple roll with a tender middle and practical crust, perfect for stew, cheese, or a meal eaten while walking. It is modest bread, but modest bread has carried more workers through a day than any royal feast.
flavorText: “A roll fits the hand because hunger rarely waits for plates.”

name: pullman loaf
shortDescription: A pullman loaf is squared, tidy, and baked for neat slices that behave on a tray. Inns prize it because it stacks well, cuts cleanly, and makes even ordinary sandwiches look like someone planned ahead.
flavorText: “The pullman loaf is bread with corners and ambition.”

name: ciabatta loaf
shortDescription: A ciabatta loaf is broad, rustic, and full of airy pockets that catch oil, sauce, and good intentions. Its crust speaks loudly, its crumb stays open, and tavern cooks trust it with meals too wet for timid bread.
flavorText: “Ciabatta is built like a sponge that went to market proud.”

name: coburg
shortDescription: A coburg loaf is round, sturdy, and marked with a confident split across the top. It looks old-fashioned in the best way, the sort of bread that belongs beside thick soup, village gossip, and honest butter.
flavorText: “A coburg does not need decoration; the crust has already signed it.”

name: muffin
shortDescription: A muffin is a small domed treat, soft inside and quick to vanish from breakfast trays. Bakers like its forgiving shape; children like that it seems like cake pretending to be respectable.
flavorText: “A muffin is what a loaf becomes when it wants applause before noon.”

name: kidney beans
shortDescription: Kidney beans are deep red, filling, and useful in pots that must feed more people than the cook expected. They store well, travel well, and turn humble stew into something with backbone.
flavorText: “Beans are cheap until winter proves them wise.”

name: pinto beans
shortDescription: Pinto beans are speckled, hearty, and dependable enough to earn space in nearly any caravan sack. They take seasoning kindly, stretch meat farther, and comfort anyone who has learned that roads are mostly waiting between meals.
flavorText: “Pinto beans are not glamorous, which is why they never disappoint.”

name: black beans
shortDescription: Black beans are small, dark, and rich when cooked slowly, turning plain pots into meals with quiet depth. They are valued by sailors, farmers, and innkeepers because flavor that stores well is almost a miracle.
flavorText: “Black beans do their best work after the impatient have left the kitchen.”

name: coffee beans
shortDescription: Coffee beans are roasted seeds with a bitter perfume strong enough to change the mood of an entire room. Scribes, guards, merchants, and exhausted parents all understand why wakefulness can be traded like spice.
flavorText: “Coffee beans are tiny brown bells rung inside the skull.”

name: black tea
shortDescription: Black tea is dark, brisk, and reliable, packed for households that prefer ceremony even when mornings are cruel. It travels better than gossip and restores composure more cheaply than a new plan.
flavorText: “Black tea is patience boiled until it speaks clearly.”

name: green tea
shortDescription: Green tea is lighter and grassy, with a clean bitterness that rewards careful brewing and punishes neglect. Healers praise it, scholars sip it, and merchants sell it best to people who want calm with their alertness.
flavorText: “Green tea does not wake a person loudly; it opens the shutters.”

name: hops
shortDescription: Hops are papery green cones with a sharp, bitter fragrance beloved by brewers and misunderstood by nearly everyone else. They give ale its bite, help it keep, and make fields smell like future taverns.
flavorText: “Hops teach sweetness discipline.”

name: butter
shortDescription: Butter is pale, rich, and dangerously persuasive in any kitchen with bread nearby. It improves sauces, pastries, vegetables, and morale, provided it is kept cool and protected from cooks with spoons.
flavorText: “Butter is proof that milk had grander ambitions.”

name: mozarella cheese
shortDescription: Mozarella cheese is soft, pale, and mild, prized for melting into long strings and making simple bread feel generous. It is best sold fresh, before its gentle sweetness dulls into ordinary dairy regret.
flavorText: “Mozarella does not shout; it stretches the conversation.”

name: feta cheese
shortDescription: Feta cheese is white, crumbly, and salty enough to brighten greens, bread, and dull afternoons. It keeps its character even in small pieces, which makes it useful to cooks who need sharpness without ceremony.
flavorText: “Feta breaks apart easily and improves everything anyway.”

name: notable gorgonzola cheese
shortDescription: Notable gorgonzola cheese is marbled, pungent, and far too confident to share a shelf quietly. Admirers call it complex; enemies call it evidence; merchants call it profitable if wrapped with conviction.
flavorText: “Gorgonzola is cheese that insists the room develop an opinion.”

name: notable muenster cheese
shortDescription: Notable muenster cheese is smooth, creamy, and washed into a bold aroma that arrives before the knife. It melts beautifully, travels cautiously, and rewards buyers who know strength can hide under softness.
flavorText: “Muenster smiles gently while making itself impossible to ignore.”

name: scarce smoked gruyere cheese
shortDescription: Scarce smoked gruyere cheese is nutty, firm, and kissed by smoke until it tastes like a mountain lodge with a good cellar. Chefs prize it for depth; thieves prize it because expensive cheese still fits in a cloak.
flavorText: “Smoke gives gruyere a memory of fire without burning the milk.”

name: yellow swiss cheese
shortDescription: Yellow swiss cheese is mild, nutty, and full of round holes that make children suspicious and cooks delighted. It slices cleanly, melts politely, and looks cheerful even when bought for purely practical reasons.
flavorText: “The holes in swiss cheese are not missing value; they are part of the charm.”

name: cheddar cheese
shortDescription: Cheddar cheese is firm, yellow, and dependable, with a sharpness that grows louder as it ages. It is tavern cheese, pantry cheese, road cheese, and the reason many apples are eaten with greater respect.
flavorText: “Cheddar is what milk becomes when it decides to have opinions.”

name: white swiss cheese
shortDescription: White swiss cheese is pale, nutty, and gently sweet, with a clean look that suits cold platters and careful lunches. Its holes give it character, though no cheesemonger has ever let them lower the price.
flavorText: “White swiss keeps its manners even when melted.”

name: notable gouda cheese
shortDescription: Notable gouda cheese is smooth, golden, and sweetly nutty, aging into crystals that crunch like tiny secrets. It sells well to travelers who want comfort and nobles who want comfort to sound imported.
flavorText: “Gouda turns patience into sweetness with a knifeable rind.”

name: notable brie cheese
shortDescription: Notable brie cheese is soft, creamy, and wrapped in a pale rind that hides its richness with practiced innocence. It belongs beside fruit, delicate knives, and guests who pretend not to notice when it runs.
flavorText: “Brie is cheese pretending to be a secret door.”

name: white egg
shortDescription: A white egg is simple, fragile, and full of kitchen possibility. It can become breakfast, batter, sauce, or supper, provided no one lets the crate meet a pothole too enthusiastically.
flavorText: “An egg is the smallest sealed bargain between hunger and chance.”

name: brown egg
shortDescription: A brown egg has the same quiet promise as any good egg, dressed in a warmer shell that market shoppers insist means something. Cooks care less about the color than whether it arrives whole.
flavorText: “Brown shells do not improve an omelet, but they do improve a basket.”

name: milk
shortDescription: Milk is fresh, pale, and always in a hurry to become something else if neglected. It feeds children, softens bread, enriches kitchens, and makes dairy merchants watch the weather like priests watch omens.
flavorText: “Milk is abundance with a short temper.”

name: olive oil
shortDescription: Olive oil is golden-green, smooth, and valuable in kitchens, lamps, salves, and trade ledgers alike. A good bottle carries orchard sun in liquid form and makes even plain bread feel intentionally served.
flavorText: “Olive oil is sunshine pressed until it learns to pour.”

name: vinegar
shortDescription: Vinegar is sharp, useful, and born from drink that took a different path. Cooks use it to brighten food, preservers use it to save harvests, and healers use it because sour things often seem trustworthy.
flavorText: “Vinegar is wine after it stopped flattering anyone.”

name: chocolate cake
shortDescription: Chocolate cake is dark, sweet, and rich enough to make ordinary celebration feel planned. It is fragile cargo, but the right buyer forgives crumbs, dents, and most moral failures once the first slice is cut.
flavorText: “Chocolate cake is diplomacy with frosting.”

name: donut
shortDescription: A donut is fried dough with a cheerful hole and a talent for disappearing before work begins. It sells best warm, sugared, and close enough to the street that passersby can smell their own lack of discipline.
flavorText: “A donut proves a circle can be both empty and satisfying.”

name: pie
shortDescription: A pie hides fruit, meat, cream, or invention under a browned crust and dares the buyer to wait. It travels badly if careless, sells quickly if fragrant, and makes every knife feel ceremonial.
flavorText: “Pie is a secret with pastry over it.”
